content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer array numsnumsnums.
An element in the array is called a **leader** if it is **strictly larger than every element to its right**. The last element in the array is always considered a leader.

Return a list of all such leaders, preserving the order in which they appear in the original array.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindLeadersfindLeadersfindLeaders – This function identifies all leader elements in the given array.

An element is considered a **leader** if it is strictly greater than every element to its right.

The last element of the array is always a leader.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A reference to an integer array of size nnn.
Return Value
Returns a list of all leader elements **preserving their order** as in the original array.
Constraints
1≤nums.length≤1051 \leq nums.length \leq 10^51≤nums.length≤105
−104≤nums[i]≤104-10^4 \leq nums[i] \leq 10^4−104≤nums[i]≤104

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains an integer nnn — the size of the array.
The second line contains nnn integers representing the array numsnumsnums.




Output Format

For each test case, print all leader elements in the same order they appear in the array, separated by spaces.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 15), the size of the array. The second line contains n space‑separated integers (‑10⁴ ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10⁴).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output all leader elements in the order they appear in the original array, separated by a single space. Print a newline after each test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Scanning from right: 2 is always a leader. 5 > 2, so leader. 8 > 5, leader. 10 > 8, leader. Elements 7 and 3 are not strictly greater than the maximum to their right.
Rightmost 1 is a leader. 4 > 1, leader. 9 > 4, leader. 6 and -2 are not greater than 9.
A single element array: the only element is trivially a leader.

solution.approach: Traverse the array from right to left while keeping the maximum value seen so far (maxFromRight). The rightmost element is always a leader. For each element nums[i] (going leftwards) if nums[i] > maxFromRight, it is a leader; store it and update maxFromRight. The collected leaders are in reverse order, so reverse them before output. This runs in O(n) time and O(1) extra space per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, where n is the size of the array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the output list.

code.Python3:
import sys

def find_leaders(arr):
    max_right = arr[-1]
    leaders = [max_right]
    for x in reversed(arr[:-1]):
        if x > max_right:
            leaders.append(x)
            max_right = x
    leaders.reverse()
    return leaders

data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
it = iter(data)
T = int(next(it))
out_lines = []
for _ in range(T):
    n = int(next(it))
    arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
    out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, find_leaders(arr))))
print('\n'.join(out_lines))
